text: The Friends of the SPCO is a nonprofit organization committed to the
support of The Saint Paul Chamber Orchestra through music
education, community awareness, audience development and
The Friends of the Saint Paul Chamber Orchestra is an independent
volunteer organization whose purpose is to support and promote the Saint
Paul Chamber Orchestra. It was organized from the Women's Philharmonic
Association in 1977 and was incorporated in 1984.
The Board of Directors of the Friends is a working board with significant
responsibilities. Board members accomplish the mission and goals of the
Friends through assignments on working committees as well as serving on
the full Board. The committees function continuously through individual
assignments, telephone and email communications and additional small
group meetings. The chairperson of each committee writes a report
containing pertinent information about the event which becomes part of the
whole committee report at the Annual Meeting.
Each Board member is important to the accomplishment of the Friends
mission and goals. Each member serves a two-year term with a maximum
of six consecutive years. Board members need to be able to devote effort
and commitment to their positions on the Board, including paying their
yearly dues by early September, regular attendance at Board meetings and
serving on at least one committee. The areas to choose among include:
Education, Membership, Special Events, Public Relations, Website
and Youth Chamber Music Competition. Board members are encouraged to
purchase a season ticket package to the SPCO concerts and to share their
Fanfare and concert experiences with friends, as well as other events

Minutes of the Annual Meeting of the Friends of the SPCO 2017–2018
Attendees: Donna Ahrens, Betty Andrews, Jo Bailey, Lynne Beck, Kay Bendel, Rick Bendel, Inez
Bergquist, Carole Boyum, Donna Bruhl, Martin Bruhl, Elizabeth Buschor, Cecil Chally, Penny
Chally, Annette Conklin, Mary Cunningham, John Donnelly, Barbara Fritz, Joan Gacki, Inez
Gantz, Karen Golden, Molly Henke, JoAnne Hill, Jean Humphries, Mary Indritz, Carolynn
Johnson, Roberta Kagin, Janet Kampf, Eric King, Debbie Koop, Gail LaFave, Ellen Lillemoen,
Corinne McCarthy, Andrea McCue, Sue Metzger, Ann Miller, Patricia O’Gorman, Mary Orr,
Barbara Osadcky, Susan Pugh, Betty Reichert, Inge Schwochau, Joy Sorich, Voni Swenson, Lola
May Thompson, Susan Travis, Linda van Dooijeweert, Dona Woolfrey
Lynne Beck called the Annual Meeting of the Friends of the SPCO to order and welcomed all
attending this luncheon. The minutes of the June 7, 2017, Annual Meeting were approved as
printed. These 2018 Annual Meeting minutes will have “readers” read and correct them before
presentation at the 2019 Annual Meeting, in keeping with the concept established at the 2017
Barb Osadcky presented the report. The total assets as of May 31, 2018, are $35,255. The
Friends will be able to give a $22,000 gift to the SPCO due to the success of our events. Barb
thanked Kay Bendel for her assistance and the committees for the great job they did with
Lynne Beck and Donna Ahrens thanked the Mendakota Country Club and the Annual Meeting
Committee for the wonderful event today. They also thanked the 2017-18 Committee and
Event Chairs for their hard work and success over this year. Donna remarked that it is a
pleasure to work with such active, intelligent, and committed volunteers in the Friends.
Andrea McCue introduced Karen’s husband John and daughter Anne, who attended today and
presented a remembrance of Karen. Andrea started the Conversation with Books with Karen,
and the Donnellys hosted a wine tasting, a holiday auction, and parties at their home over their
many years as Friends. Karen put the “fun” in fundraising and was the detail person who
brought joy to her work and did so effortlessly. We shall miss her.
Donna Ahrens invited everyone to take a seashell in Karen’s memory. We have received very
generous donations in Karen’s memory and they will go to support our work.
Announcement of the Friend par Excellence Awards
Betty Andrews and Andrea McCue each received the Friend par Excellence Award. Betty has
done both large and small things for the Friends over her years as a member, bringing
refreshments for the Freeman lectures and Youth Competition, hosting Board meetings,
“showing up” to Friends events, and encouraging leaders and volunteers with her words and
actions. Andrea has been a dedicated Friend for many years, is willing to do everything, has
chaired the Bylaws Committee, decorates for events, hosts musicians, and helped start
Conversation with Books. She is always here, always volunteering.
Donna Ahrens recognized Katie Berg, the SPCO’s director of development, and Jon Limbacher,
SPCO managing director and president, for their dedication to the SPCO and their extraordinary
support of the Friends through their exceptional service, time, and enthusiasm. Donna
presented them with a print of “The Musicians” which was donated by former SPCO
concertmaster Romuald Tecco 20 years ago for a Friends gala auction. The print was purchased
by longtime Friends member Louise King and Louise’s daughter returned it to the Friends upon
Louise’s death a few years ago. It seemed very fitting to present it to the SPCO as it continues
to expand its mission to work with kids and the community to make its music accessible.
Vote on Changes to The Friends’ Bylaws
Lynne Beck thanked Andrea McCue and her committee for preparing the bylaws changes
presented today. The motion to approve the bylaws changes in full was seconded and passed.
The motion to accept the bylaws changes as distributed today was seconded and passed.
Nominations and Vote on the Slate of Officers, Committee Chairs, and Board Members
Penny Chally thanked the committee and presented the slate as distributed and Lynne Beck
asked for any nominations from the floor. Nominations were closed and Lynne asked to have
the slate approved as printed – the motion was made, seconded, and passed. New Board
members were then welcomed and presented with a rose. Retiring Board members Betty
Andrews, Andrea McCue, and Susan Pugh were given thanks and farewell, along with a rose.
Jon Limbacher, Managing Director and President of the SPCO, gave an update on the SPCO and
said speaking at the Friends’ luncheon is his favorite thing he has to do. Jon thanked Lynne
Beck and Donna Ahrens for their incredible leadership, as they are great assets who enjoy their
work with the Friends and with the SPCO. Jon then asked all the former Friends’ presidents to
stand and be recognized in appreciation. Jon acknowledged Katie Berg, who raises 60% of the
SPCO revenues through her fundraising work. He feels she is emblematic of the rest of the staff;
this is the best staff in the country for orchestras. Jon loves the Friends and feels we are
essential in providing a sense of family, which is the resiliency of this organization.
There is no other city, outside of New York City, that supports two orchestras like we do in the
Twin Cities, and the Friends work with Jon and his staff together to build this wonderful SPCO
organization. There is consistency of great concerts each and every time; the SPCO is dynamic,
interesting, and has the most dedicated musicians in all the world right now. The SPCO is
striving to be accessible to the community with pricing, to include whole families, through new
concert venues in Humboldt High School and other local locations. Jon emphasized that it is not
enough to play for a small segment of the community; rather, there is a goal to share it with all
the community. To that point, in May the SPCO mission statement was expanded to add the
organization’s commitment to accessibility and inclusion of community members.
Penny Chally presented Lynne Beck and Donna Ahrens with roses for their year as co-presidents
and for their wonderful leadership as they “cheered us on and cheered us up.” Penny then
presented Mary Cunningham with a small rose bush to welcome her as the new co-president
with Donna. Donna presented Lynne with an outgoing gift and her thanks for her work this past
Annette Conklin reported on just returning from Italy, having traveled with the three 14-year-
old musicians and their families to the Chamber Music Festival in Mantova. The young
musicians performed three times and ironically, performed in the hall where Mozart performed
when he was 14 years old. They all had a wonderful time. Annette thanked the members of the
Friends who generously donated to help defer some of these families’ travel expenses; $1000
Donna Ahrens stated the Annual Report will be posted in the next month or so, as the website
is in the process of being redeveloped by Joan Gacki and her committee. The meeting was
Proposed Internal Audit Procedures: This is done yearly
The task of the audit committee is to verify that the financial information reported to the
membership and on the tax return is accurate and complete. This involves verifying the annual
report information, cash and investment account balances as reported in the general ledger
and the carry forward to the next year of the designated fund balances as reported in the
general ledger. In addition, the audit committee should examine the accounting procedures
and internal controls for weaknesses. This is accomplished using the following procedures:
v A random sampling of deposits should be selected to determine whether or not income
has been reported correctly in the general ledger and that the deposits are made to
the bank in a timely and accurate manner.
v Verify that the general ledger prepared by the Treasurer matches the financial
v All balance sheet accounts in the general ledger should be verified. For example, all
bank accounts should be reconciled using the bank statements, the general ledger and
the bank reconciliations prepared by the Treasurer. Investment accounts should be
v Restricted Fund balances reflect the amounts of designated monies carried over from
one year to the next. Any additions to the restricted funds should be verified.
v Expenditures from restricted funds should be verified. Parameters of the restricted
fund should be consulted to be sure expenditures meet the qualifications of the
v The Treasurer’s Report should be compared to the approved budget and significant
v Other procedures may be performed as deemed necessary by the committee to insure
v In addition to the above specific activities, the audit committee should be mindful of
the processes and procedures used in the day-to-day financial dealings of the
organization. These processes should be reviewed for possible inadequate separation
of duties, timeliness and overall “best practices.” Weaknesses or suggestions for
improvement should be part of the committee’s report.
Internal Audit of the Friends of The SPCO
In attendance: Donna Ahrens, Lynne Beck, Kay Bendel, Annette Conklin, Mary Cunningham, Barbara
Osadcky and Betty Reichert. Penny Chally was unable to attend.
Kay and Barbara opened the meeting by describing the accounts.
Two bank accounts: 1) a savings account holding the restricted funds and 2) the checking account that
serves the operations of both the Friends management and the Competition.
There are two spreadsheets – one provided by Barbara for the operating income and expenses and one
maintained by Kay for the activities of the Competition.
All checks are written by Barbara. Both Barbara and Kay make deposits.
Several items of entry in the spreadsheets were questioned and were verified by Barbara.
The bank balance was confirmed in agreement with the Balance sheet.
All were approved for accuracy in amount.
As always, several entries were questioned regarding the category in which they were placed, and
several were requested to be moved into different categories.
The final check for $22,515 and was presented to the SPCO by June 30.
The report failed to specify the amount generated from the Treasure Sale and therefore restricted for
The committee asked Barbra to provide that information to the presidents.
A motion was made by Lynne Beck and seconded by Donna Ahrens to “declare the audit thorough,
complete and approved.” Motion was passed by unanimous vote.

chairperson of the Competition committee and of the Events committee, and an at-large member of the
Recommendations are sent to the Officers of the Friends of the SPCO.
Kay Bendel, Chair of the Finance Committee
Memorials to the SPCO from the Friends
TOTAL CASH GIFT TO THE SPCO 6/30/18
This year brought old and new Friends together to offer numerous opportunities in support of
the Saint Paul Chamber Orchestra’s mission and its musicians. Our fundraising resulted in an
annual gift of $22,000 to the SPCO. In addition, we contributed more than $500 in memorials
for several Friends. Our report covers only the highlights of a rewarding year of events and
In June 2018, Donna attended the three-day 73rd National Conference of the League of
American Orchestras, held in Chicago. She concentrated on attending sessions for members of
volunteer organizations such as the Friends of the SPCO. The sessions offered a wide range of
presentations and discussions on various aspects of volunteer organizations, including
education, fundraising events, and attracting new members. The sessions were informative and
provided a wealth of ideas to bring back to the Friends, as well as opportunities to meet and
talk with members of volunteer groups from across the nation.
At a potluck gathering in August, hosted by Lynne Beck, members of the Friends board and
their spouses shared food and conversation. Old and new members had time to get acquainted
before the official beginning of another busy year of Friends meetings and events.
In September, the Friends hosted a Welcome Back Luncheon at the Landmark Center for the
musicians and staff of the SPCO. This tradition of more than 25 years gives the Friends the
opportunity to help kick off the SPCO’s new season and connect with musicians over a catered
lunch. In June, the Friends hosted an Appreciation Luncheon to toast the musicians and
celebrate the close of another successful season.
A new event this year was a style show, which was held at the University Club in St. Paul in
October and drew nearly 100 attendees. A luncheon was served, and about a dozen members
of the Friends modeled clothing from Chico’s, to great applause. This fundraising event was so
successful that the Friends board agreed that it should be offered again next year.
In January, we sponsored a demonstration of the SPCO’s Concert Library. Launched in
December 2017, the SPCO offers its Concert Library programs online, without charge, to make
their music more accessible and reach more people in our community, in their homes and in
institutions such as senior living facilities, neighborhood centers, schools, and hospitals. Fifteen
members of the Friends attended a two-hour presentation by SPCO staff members Matt
Thueson, Erin Jude, and Kate Nordstrum, who provided a history of the planning for the
Concert Library, shared their observations about its implementation, and demonstrated how to
access it and choose from the musical selections.
Later in January, an Evening with Musicians, hosted by Andrea and Steve McCue, drew a full
house of Friends. The 25 attendees enjoyed a delightful evening of music by SPCO violinists
Maureen Nelson and Eunice Kim, along with hors d’oeuvres, wine and conversation.
The Youth Chamber Music Competition semifinals and finals were held in March at Hamline
University’s Sundin Hall. Chuck Ullery, recently retired principal bassoonist for the SPCO, served
as emcee. First place and Grand Prize went to the Fourzando String Quartet, whose members
played prior to the Friends-sponsored SPCO concert on May 19. A post-concert reception was
held for those who sponsored, volunteered for, and supported the Competition.
Another highlight was the Music at the Museum event, in April, which for the first time was
held at the Museum of Russian Art, in Minneapolis. Approximately 170 people attended the
event, which featured SPCO musicians Ruggero Allifranchini, Maiya Papach, and Julie Albers.
Our popular longtime annual events, including the Daniel Freeman Lectures series and
Conversation with Books, again offered musical and literary insights to Friends members, guests
Throughout the year, the Friends continued its tradition of providing volunteers for the SPCO’s
education programming for youth. The programs are now offered at sites in North Minneapolis
and the West Side of St. Paul, as well as at the Ordway. We are dedicated to supporting the
SPCO’s efforts to expand its reach to young people throughout the metropolitan areas.
At the Friends Annual Meeting on June 5, 2018, Betty Andrews and Andrea McCue received the
Friend Par Excellence Award. Betty has been active for years in supporting many Friends events,
as well as providing space at her condo for Friends monthly meetings. Andrea has been a
dedicated Friend for many years, volunteering for countless events. She has chaired the Bylaws
Committee, hosted evenings with musicians, and co-chaired Conversation with Books. The
Friends also recognized the SPCO’s support of the Friends organization. We presented Jon
Limbacher and Katie Berg with a print that was donated to a Friends SPCO auction 20 years ago
The Friends continued to update its printed materials, Facebook pages and website. The
Publicity Committee began working with a website consultant on a makeover that will be both
more engaging (for example, by allowing visitors to the site to make donations and purchase
memberships and tickets online) and easier for Friends publicity chairs to navigate.
Throughout this busy year, members of the Friends devoted their time and energy as
committee chairs, board members, and volunteers. As Co-Presidents, we have been impressed
with your expertise, work ethic, and energy and are honored to have served with you in this
enthusiastic and hard-working group. Thank you for all you do!

The topic for our fall series was “Ravel, the Elegant Impressionist.” The average attendance for
those classes in October and November was 22. Two participants signed up as new members of
The topic for the winter series in January was “Verdi’s Rigaletto: a Tale of Passion, Betrayal and
Vengeance.” The average attendance was 21. Seven new names were added to the lecture
series mailing list, hopefully as future participants.
The spring series topic was “A Schubert Sampler.” Average attendance for the April series was
All classes were held in the Kirk Parlor at House of Hope Church on Wednesdays, at 10:00
AM. The church staff took care of all set-up needs and provided coffee, tea and serving
equipment. Treats were provided by class members.
Daniel’s lectures were consistently informative, interesting and fun. They were supported by
printed handouts as well as videos, when needed. Every class was a great pleasure for all and
well worth the challenges presented by winter and spring weather.
Mary Patton and Ellen Lillemoen, Co-Chairs
The Friends of the St. Paul Chamber Orchestra again played an integral part in the orchestra’s
education outreach. An enthusiastic and dedicated group of Friends volunteers were on hand
to help with eight Start the Music programs and four Xplorchestra events. At these fun events,
volunteers performed a variety of duties, including face painting, making an “instrument” with
the children’s participation, showing how various instruments are played, and, in general,
making sure that the children were having a fun, informative morning as well as listening to a
Start the Music is geared to children three to six years old; Xplorchestra caters to children aged
six to twelve. The children were very excited to attend concerts featuring woodwinds, brass and
percussion instruments. Each session was held at the SPCO Center in the Hamm Building in St.
Paul. Outreach sessions at the Humboldt High School on St. Paul’s West Side, and at the Capri
Theatre in North Minneapolis, were very well attended.
Finally, on Saturday, April 14, half a dozen Friends braved a blizzard to volunteer at the first-
ever Family Arts Blast at the Ordway. The Arts Partnership (The Schubert Club, The SPCO, MN
Opera and The Ordway) sponsored this free event. Families interacted with music, dance,
theater and performances of all kinds; they could make or try an instrument, learn new dance
moves, try their hand at costume and sound design; attend a show or concert inside the two
performance spaces; and much more. Despite the challenging weather, the event was a great
success as a way to engage new audience members and share free arts offerings with families
In addition to these events, the Friends also volunteered at a variety of Friends and SPCO
activities as needed. It was a very good year!
Updated contact information for our state and national elected ofﬁcials was distributed to all
board members with the admonition to keep it in a very accessible location and to use it often.
It works for all issues, not only Arts issues.
Lynne Beck attended Arts Advocacy Day at the Minnesota State Capitol on March 6. Inclement
weather kept some people home, but a hearty 250 advocates came and talked to legislators.
Lynne’s group met with two legislators and left packets for four more who were unable to
meet. They delivered the message that the arts have a big impact on communities around the
state, and each group highlighted the state funds that have supported arts organizations and
artists in their district. They also thanked the legislators for their support of the arts and the
47% of Legacy funds that are designated for the Minnesota State Arts Board and Regional Arts
Minnesota Citizens for the Arts (MCA) is the oldest and most successful state arts advocacy
organization in the nation. Because of MCA, Minnesota is the only state with arts funding
written into the state constitution. The Legacy Amendment, passed on Arts Advocacy Day 2008,
ensures 25 years of funding for the nonproﬁt arts.
Yearly duties of the Membership Committee are to update and maintain a data base with
current members, encourage new memberships, encourage past members to rejoin, collect
and record donations made for membership, send contribution acknowledgments to
members, provide labels and email addresses for Friends mailings, and work with the
Special Events committee on events to benefit members and increase membership.
The start of this fiscal year for the Friends began with Renewal Request Letters
being sent in late August. We continue to accept memberships online through PayPal
as well as by check. The membership categories continue at three levels: Individual/Household,
$60; Sponsor, $100; and Benefactor, $150+. While there are no specific “perks” to giving
at higher levels, it appears most organizations of our type do the same, and having
suggested donation levels appears to increase giving.
Our total number of members is 174 with 13 new or returning former members. A
change was made on how our members are counted: Instead of counting member
families, we are counting each member of that family (e.g., Penny and Cecil Chally = 2
The committee continues to work on increasing members, and continues to work with the
Special Events Committee on events that benefit both existing members and prospective
members (for example, Evenings with Musicians and the Style Show). These events continue to

Seven Friends Special Events were held this year:
September 19 Welcome Back Luncheon for musicians and staff, held in Courtroom 317 in
Landmark Center. Chaired by Mary McDiarmid and Betty Reichert.
October 26 Style Show in collaboration with Chico’s held at the University Club, St. Paul.
Chaired by CJ Suchta. Attendance was over 90; profit was $1,975.
February 5 Evening with Musicians hosted by Andrea and Steve McCue. Maureen Nelson and
Eunice Kim were the featured musicians. Attendance was 24.
April 24 Musicians at the Museum held at the Russian Museum in Minneapolis.
Chaired by Mary McDiarmid, Betty Reichert, Carolynn Johnson, CJ Suchta and Joan Gacki.
May 17 Conversation with Books chaired by Kay Bendel and Andrea McCue and held in the Kirk
Parlor of House of Hope. Attendance 76; profit was $1,055. See report on the following page.
June 5 Annual Luncheon at the Mendakota Club in Mendota Heights, chaired by Lola Mae
June 7 Appreciation Luncheon to celebrate the end of season with musicians and staff.
Attendance was approximately 90. Chaired by Mary McDiarmid and Betty Reichert.
The 19th Annual “Conversation with Books” was held at The House of Hope Presbyterian Church
on May 17, 2018. Invitations were designed by Joan Gacki with a photo of the books provided
by St. Catherine University. We went back to the letter style invitation with a reply envelope
enclosed and all were mailed first class postage. Andrea covered the printing of 300 invitations
of which about 220 were mailed to members of the Friends and to guests who have attended
previous years. The remainder were distributed to Board members to hand out or self-mail to
friends. Voni Swenson staffed the check-in table and Karen Golden greeted all guests as they
arrived. Seventy-six people were present at the event and, as always, they were a very
enthusiastic and appreciative audience. Admission was $25 per person and $20 for members of
the Friends. Joan Gacki provided great email publicity blasts to encourage attendance. Many,
many thanks to the many Friends who attended and brought guests to this event.
Co-president Lynne Beck welcomed guests and told them about the Friends organization. Kay
Bendel gave a bit of history about the event and introduced the panel.
The four-women panel from St. Catherine University led an entertaining and stimulating
discussion of nine books. The panel distributed copies of the list of the books discussed plus
Excelsior Bay Books, a privately owned local bookstore, provided books for sale giving a
percentage of the receipts to the Friends. Susan Metzger, Rick Bendel and Lilly Bendel-Stenzel
efficiently staffed the book sale table.
Andrea McCue beautifully coordinated and graciously hosted the reception table. Delicious
treats, nuts, and fruit were donated by Rick Bendel, Penny Chally, Andrea McCue, Barbara
Osadcky, Betty Reichert, Sherry Spence and Voni Swenson. Flowers from Andrea decorated the
Profit: $1,000 + $55.44 profit from the book sales = $1,055.44
There were eleven applications to the 2018 competition—one in Creative, one in Level B, and
nine in Level A. Three did not advance beyond the Video round.
Seven groups performed in the Finalists Recital on Sunday, March 18, including the winners in
the Creative Category, the Wind category, and in Level B The judges awarded one First place,
two Second places and a Third place prize.
First place and Grand Prize went to the Fourzando String Quartet, coached by Tom Rosenberg,
director of the Saint Paul Conservatory of Music Chamber Music Academy. They performed in
the Ordway on Saturday, May 19, preceding the Friends-sponsored SPCO concert. The
competition again hosted a reception on the second-tier for winners and their parents, donors,
volunteers, advisory board members, and SPCO musicians and staff. I met with two SPCO staff
members, who ordered the same menu we had last year from Lake Elmo Inn. We had the Inn
provide the coffee, as it cost far less than coffee from the Ordway.
Many thanks are due to Becky Cline and Kate Ailaabouni from the SPCO development office, for
ordering and setting up the cheese, fruit, and desserts. Many of the musicians came, as well as
Jonathan Cohen, artistic director and harpsichordist, and Jon and Patti Limbacher.
The Level B winners, Trio Luminoso, will participate in a Mantova Chamber Music Festival in
Mantua, Italy. On June 1, among other activities, they will perform the entire Beethoven Piano
Trio in D, Op. 7 in two or three schools, and attend a master class led by Alfred Brendel. The
advisory board of the competition voted not to support the travel of the students from the
competition’s treasury. Consequently, five generous donors—three of them Friends of the
SPCO—contributed to give each family $1,000 toward their travel expenses.
The preliminary estimated financial report prepared by Kay Bendel shows that total income was
$16,019.75. Estimated expenses are about $18,300. The final report will be available in June.
The competition account’s balance is estimated to be about $14,800.
A competition wrap-up wine, appetizer and dessert celebration for the competition’s
volunteers was held at my home on Thursday evening, May 10. Thirteen attendees contributed
delicious food and wine. The meeting was very productive and a fine time was had by all.
Thanks to all the Friends who worked on the competition. You were great!!
Because there have never been more than 12 ensembles entering the competition, the
Advisory Board voted to eliminate the Semifinal round next year. A committee from the
Advisory Board—Chuck Ullery, Mina Fisher, Fred Sewell and I—will meet on June 12 at my
home to re-examine the competition’s guidelines and bring them up to date for 2019.
The Finalists Recital will be on a Saturday next year — February 23, 2019 —and will be held
again at Sundin Hall, Hamline University.
I still need a co-chair for the 2019 competition. Sherry Spence will assist with mailings and non-
computer-related tasks, for which I am very grateful.
